Assuming universals without checking exceptions leads to semantic errors and scrambled sentences.

nyh:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translation to head-only or dependent-only marking languages can result in redundant or missing agreement markers, breaking syntactic coherence and agreement consistency. (symbol: mark:double-head-dep) Exception: analytic target languages that drop agreement (e.g., English) require explicit clarification but avoid over-marking issues  SYMBOLS [past:present-form]=Past tense expressed via present-tense morphology, causing potential misinterpretation of tense if translator assumes present form equals present time. [past:remoteness-infer]=Past tense morphology encodes 2–3 degrees of temporal remoteness, forcing inference of distance from context when source lacks such distinctions. [past:remoteness]=Past time expressed by present-tense morphology with two-to-three degrees of temporal remoteness; translation into languages lacking remoteness grading may collapse distinct past nuances into a single past tense, losing temporal precision. [optative:present-form]=Inflectional optative mood marked on present-tense verbs; form identical to indicative present risks confusion between wish and statement. [optative:context-infer]=Inflectional optative requires inference of optative meaning (wish/hope) from context when source language lacks dedicated optative. [imperative:number-infer]=Imperative morphology distinguishes 2sg/2pl, forcing inference of addressee number from context when source does not mark it. [prohibitive:imp+neg]=Prohibitive formed by normal imperative + special negative particle; requires decomposition of single-word prohibitives from source languages. [neg:bound-expand]=Bound-negative morpheme whose surface position varies with subject, object and verb. [perf:other-type]=“Other” perfect aspect that does not align with canonical present-perfect or past-perfect morphologies. [dio:double-obj]=Ditransitive “give” realized as a double-object construction (two bare objects, no preposition). [dio:indirect-obj]=Ditransitive “give” expressed with an indirect-object pattern (prepositional phrase for recipient). [proh:imp-neg]=Prohibitive formed by a normal imperative plus a dedicated negative morpheme; in languages that encode prohibition via a distinct morpheme or lexical item, a literal “imperative+negative” may be interpreted as a simple negated command rather than a prohibition. [ord:suffix-th]=Ordinal numerals formed by attaching the suffix -th to cardinal bases, overlapping with a strongly suffixing morphological profile (suffix competition). [neg:post-verb]=Immediate post-verbal negative particle (negation follows the verb) contrasting with the prevalent preverbal negative pattern in many languages. [suf:neg-collision]=Simultaneous presence of strongly suffixing inflectional morphology and immediate preverbal negative particles. [imp:2sg-2pl]=Distinct imperative affixes for second-person singular vs. plural; translating into languages without number-distinguished imperatives may erase the addressee-number information. [mark:double-head-dep]=Double marking on both clause head and dependents (head-marking + dependent-marking) in a strongly suffixing morphology environment.

bgg:
[SYNTAX]
- Source flexibility is replaced by a statistically likely but linguistically unmotivated target order; pragmatic effects of source variation are lost; translation engines may arbitrarily place adjectives, leading to unnatural phrasing or semantic mis‑attachment. (symbol: bgg>>adj-n:no-dom)
bxu:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Ambiguity between possessive and deictic pre-nominal modifiers; translating possessive pronouns into Buriat requires selecting a deictic value (proximal/distal) not present in source, adding unintended spatial information; translating out of Buriat loses the deictic nuance embedded in the possessive form. (symbol: pro-dem:stem:merge)
gvl:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- When translating from an SOV or VSO language, the translator must force an SVO reorder to preserve the negation pattern, which can alter information structure, shift focus, or produce ungrammatical negation if any of the three negative slots is misplaced. (symbol: neg:triple-slot+SVO)
mhu:
[SYNTAX]
- Translation to languages with Numeral-Noun order requires structural reordering. (symbol: [mhu]>>num:post)
jaq:
[PRAGMATICS]
- Forced collapse to generic 3rd person loses non-human reference tracking; downstream anaphora resolution degrades. (symbol: [jaq]>>dem:nonhuman-link)
spt:
[SYNTAX]
- Zero-shot translation places numeral before noun incorrectly; quantifier scope and NP structure corrupted. (symbol: [spt]>>num:post-head)
cao:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- source noun phrases with adpositional phrases become ambiguous after translation, often resulting in incorrect argument labeling or omitted case markers in languages such as German or Russian. (symbol: ADP_PRON_GAP) Exception: contexts where pragmatic cues disambiguate arguments
vif:
[SYNTAX]
- Numerals follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking quantifier misplacement. (symbol: np:num-post)
kup:
[SYNTAX]
- Numerals follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking quantifier misplacement. (symbol: np:num-post)
jng:
[SYNTAX]
- Demonstratives precede noun in source but require postnominal position in target, risking deictic misplacement. (symbol: np:dem-pre)
wms:
[SYNTAX]
- Demonstratives precede noun in source but require postnominal position in target, risking deictic misplacement. (symbol: np:dem-pre)
mph:
[SYNTAX]
- Demonstratives precede noun in source but require postnominal position in target, risking deictic misplacement. (symbol: np:dem-pre)
yns:
[SYNTAX]
- Genitives follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking possessive relation reversal. (symbol: np:gen-post)
mnf:
[SYNTAX]
- Genitives follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking possessive relation reversal. (symbol: np:gen-post)
cac:
[SYNTAX]
- Genitives follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking possessive relation reversal. (symbol: np:gen-post)
sya:
[SYNTAX]
- Relative clauses follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking clause misattachment. (symbol: np:rel-post)
tzn:
[SYNTAX]
- Relative clauses follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking clause misattachment. (symbol: np:rel-post)
dow:
[SYNTAX]
- Relative clauses follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking clause misattachment. (symbol: np:rel-post)
ndv:
[SYNTAX]
- Relative clauses follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking clause misattachment. (symbol: np:rel-post)
bom:
[SYNTAX]
- Relative clauses follow noun in source but require prenominal position in target, risking clause misattachment. (symbol: np:rel-post)
tyx:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Vowel alternations in morphological paradigms may not map to target vowel systems, causing phonological distortion and potential lexical meaning change. (symbol: vow:inventory-large)
wsi:
[SYNTAX]
- Translating a Woisika clause with both pre-verb and post-verb negation into a language that treats double negation as reinforcement (e.g., English) will incorrectly render the statement as affirmative unless the system detects and collapses the double negation. (symbol: neg double single)
fud:
[SYNTAX]
- Automatic word-order generation may place numerals incorrectly, altering definiteness or quantity interpretation. (symbol: numeral order ambig) Exception: Contextual markers (e.g., classifiers) can sometimes reveal the intended order.
niq:
[SYNTAX]
- If actually Mixed, the same hazard as Kashmiri applies. If treated as fixed N-RC but is actually Mixed, RC-N contexts get misordered. (symbol: RC-DATA>>CONFLICT)
nau:
[SYNTAX]
- Order alternation likely tracks proximal/distal or specific/non-specific. Targets like English (Dem-N) or Thai (N-Dem) collapse this distinction unless compensated by lexical modifiers (this/that, the/a). (symbol: DEM-ORDER>>AMBIG)
svs:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Source tonal evidentials or causative markers collapse to segmental-only forms; distinctions are unrecoverable without periphrasis. Target must insert explicit markers for each lost tonal category. (symbol: NO-TONE>>MORPH-GAP)
tpy:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Tonal grammatical categories in source collapse; target requires periphrastic expansion for each lost tonal distinction. (symbol: NO-TONE>>MORPH-GAP)
niy:
[PRAGMATICS]
- Translating from Latvian/Wayampi requires three distinct hedge types ("I saw", "it seems", "they say"); Ngiti's limited pragmatic strategy conflates direct vs inferential, losing evidential precision. (symbol: NO-EVID>>HEDGE-ONLY)
waq:
[SYNTAX]
- Single verb-position flip ignores co-varying adposition, genitive, and RC orders; target output may show "SOV but prepositional" hybrids violating target grammar. (symbol: SOV>>CLUSTER-FLIP)
xaw:
[PRAGMATICS]
- Evidential nuance is either omitted or rendered with a generic adverb, reducing source‑speaker reliability cues. (symbol: evi particle loss)
shh:
[PRAGMATICS]
- Evidence marker may be omitted or mis‑interpreted, leading to inaccurate source attribution. (symbol: evi particle clash)
zmc:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translator may misinterpret possession relationships, producing incorrect genitive constructions in the target. (symbol: case_border_ambigu)
yad:
[SYNTAX]
- Target languages that separate pronouns and demonstratives may lose the built‑in deictic information, resulting in vague or incorrect reference resolution. (symbol: PRON_DEM_FUSION_HAZARD)
unn:
[PRAGMATICS]
- Translations from evidential languages (Achumawi, Mam) lose source‑of‑information marking unless periphrastic hedging (reportedly, apparently) automatically injected. (symbol: UNN>>evid:absent-loss)
kic:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translation to languages without grammatical evidentials (e.g., English) loses evidential nuance; back-translation cannot recover source evidence type; translation may omit or mis-represent the speaker’s evidential stance, leading to ambiguous or misleading statements. (symbol: evidential-particle-loss)
cea:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Machine translation from Chehalis to languages with >6 vowels may mis-map homophonous forms; ASR for Chehalis suffers high confusion; phonological neutralization obscures morphological boundaries. (symbol: vowel-inventory-homophony-risk)
aar:
[SYNTAX]
- Typological generalizations about postpositional languages contaminated by Qafar data under wrong code; MT language identification confused. (symbol: postpositions-iso-mismatch)
auc:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Homophonous words may be misinterpreted in the target language without compensatory cues; creates a risk of meaning loss when translating into tonal languages where tone distinguishes lexical items. (symbol: TONE:ABSENT:RISK) Exception: Non-tonal target languages are safe.
amy:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Substantivized adjectives (e.g., "the red one") fail if the adjective is used in isolation without the required morphological suffix. (symbol: [amy]>>adj:isolated-suffix)
anp:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Loanwords or transliterations containing /l/ cannot be realized phonologically; they must be substituted, risking loss of recognizability. (symbol: lateral:no‑/l/)
asv:
[PHONOLOGY]
- When transliterated into scripts or target languages that encode voicing, Asmat consonants may be mis-rendered, leading to spelling errors, mispronunciation, or collapse of lexical distinctions that rely on voicing in the target system. (symbol: PHONVOIC:gap)
avo:
[SYNTAX]
- Imposing a rigid S‑O‑V / O‑V pattern mis‑orders constituents, distorting information structure. (symbol: syntax:order:free‑break)
awx:
[SYNTAX]
- In languages that mark case on relative nouns post-nominally, prenominal placement obscures case relations, causing parsing errors; translators must reorder and possibly add complementizers. (symbol: RELCL:order-mismatch)
bmo:
[SYNTAX]
- English VO/prepositional phrases must be fully reversed (VP, PP, NP order); nested embedding reversal increases cognitive load and error rate in clause ordering. (symbol: [BMO]>>syn:ov:mirror-reorder) Exception: CONFIDENCE: high (ISO bmi resolved to bmo)
brp:
[SYNTAX]
- Bare NPs are ambiguous between generic, indefinite specific, and indefinite non-specific readings. Translation from languages with obligatory indefinite articles (*a/an*) must either insert a numeral *one* or leave the noun bare, losing the contrast. (symbol: brp>>art:def-only)
bso:
[SYNTAX]
- Translators who drop overt case markers (as many target languages do) lose clear identification of which argument is negated, producing ambiguous or incorrect truth‑conditions. (symbol: ARGMARK_SYM_NEG)
btr:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- In translation to languages that lack person-inflection on adpositions, the person information encoded on the adposition will be lost, reducing referential clarity; person agreement on adpositions dropped during adposition conversion. (symbol: person adp loss; adposition person loss) Exception: targets that encode person on associated pronouns can recover the information
bxe:
[PRAGMATICS]
- ambiguous translations of sentences like "the hand was broken" may be read as referring to the arm, leading to factual errors. (symbol: hand arm ambiguity)
byz:
[SYNTAX]
- Variable ordering confuses subordinate linkage in targets that expect a single, fixed order. (symbol: mixed subordinator fixed)
bzo:
[SYNTAX]
- Translating into languages that forbid double negation can unintentionally cancel negation, altering truth-value of statements; target languages without double-negation tolerance may misinterpret intended negative polarity. (symbol: NEGDOUBLE:conflict)
cbk:
[PRAGMATICS]
- Failure to retain green-blue contrast may distort descriptions of flora, clothing, or idiomatic expressions; may require descriptive workaround with risk of ambiguity. (symbol: COLOR:gran-loss)
cnt:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Person agreement on adpositional phrases may be omitted or must be re-phrased, leading to possible misidentification of arguments. (symbol: adposition-person-loss) Exception: when the adpositional phrase contains an overt pronoun
crx:
[OTHER]
- Translation into languages with hand/arm identity will result in lexical collapse. (symbol: HANDARM_DISTINCT)
csh:
[SYNTAX]
- Reordering negation morpheme in NegV targets may cause scope errors; misplaced negative morpheme leads to ungrammatical target output. (symbol: CSH NEG VNEG)
ctd:
[SYNTAX]
- High flexibility in constituent ordering requires precise tracking of grammatical roles to avoid role-reversal errors in target languages with rigid SVO/SOV orders. (symbol: ctd>>SV-OV-dual)
cup:
[SYNTAX]
- Incorrect placement of the negative morpheme can change polarity or render the clause unintelligible. (symbol: NEG_POS_ERROR)
daj:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Automatic transliteration of uvular stops from source languages into Daju will fail, resulting in missing or substituted symbols and unreadable output. (symbol: [daj]>>uvular:continuant-only)
djw:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Same as above – number‑agreement breakdown. (symbol: plural word conversion)
duq:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Possessor and possessed nouns are swapped in translation, yielding incorrect relationships or unintelligible noun phrases. (symbol: GENNOUNREV)
gdi:
[SYNTAX]
- Literal adjective strings may be rejected or misinterpreted in the target language, leading to translation failure. (symbol: adj:standalone)
gdu:
[SYNTAX]
- Negation cannot follow the verb in SOV order; must appear pre‑verbally or medially — combined with dependent possession marking on the possessed noun, negation placement may interfere with possessive suffix visibility. (symbol: neg:poss-suffix-clash)
giz:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- In translation to affixal‑plurality languages, the plural word may be misparsed as a quantifier or determiner, corrupting number agreement and DP structure; failure to generate the plural word or to attach it correctly can result in missing number information in the target translation. (symbol: PLURAL WORD MARK) Exception: none  NO NEW SYMBOLS
gnr:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Adjusting only the morpheme form or only the clause position misplaces negation scope; both must shift in tandem. (symbol: [GNR]>>neg:preverb:order-mismatch) Exception: CONFIDENCE: high (ISO gnk resolved to gnr)
grs:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Same as above – incorrect negation. (symbol: prefix negation suffix)
grv:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Duplicate or missing profile entries for Grebo in DOT; downstream lookup by ISO fails silently. (symbol: [grv]>>iso:code:fragmented) Exception: ISO 639-3 'grv' is standard.
gsw:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translating into synthetic-distributive languages requires collapsing two words into one inflected form; translating out requires expanding single word into marker+numeral phrase, risking scope ambiguity. (symbol: [GSW]>>num:dist:periph-marker) Exception: CONFIDENCE: high (ISO deu resolved to gsw)
gyy:
[OTHER]
- Same as other hand‑arm languages. (symbol: hand arm distinction)
hla:
[SYNTAX]
- Conditional negation may be dropped or added indiscriminately, producing statements that are either unintentionally affirmative or doubly negative. (symbol: neg:cond‑only)
hya:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Verb agrees with subject/object via person/number only; gender‑based agreement distinctions from source languages must be lexicalised since no morphological slot exists. (symbol: agr:genderless-kiranti)
ikk:
[SYNTAX]
- Misplaced negative morpheme leads to incorrect negation or meaning shift in the target output. (symbol: NEGORDERREV)
ilb:
[SYNTAX]
- Translation to VNeg languages requires shifting the negative operator. (symbol: ilb>>NegV-order)
itz:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Ignoring the phenomenon strips away lexical nuance (e.g., emphasis), compromising translation fidelity. (symbol: REDUP_POLY_EXP)
jeh:
[OTHER]
- Translation into languages that distinguish hand vs. arm requires contextual disambiguation or periphrasis. (symbol: HANDARM_IDENTITY)
jhi:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Substituted stops can create homophony, leading to ambiguity or incorrect meaning in the target text. (symbol: IMPLOSIVE ONLY HAZ)
kcd:
[SYNTAX]
- Loss of case cues results in incorrect agent‑patient assignment, especially in passive‑like constructions, corrupting meaning. (symbol: ARG_OVERT_MARK) Exception: none (ISO code conflict: Isolate 1 uses kcd, Isolate 2 uses kmu)
kdr:
[OTHER]
- Neither isolate code matches Kaurna; translation may default to generic term, losing precision. (symbol: ISO code conflict)
kgw:
[SYNTAX]
- Objects may be mis‑ordered after the verb, producing syntactically incorrect or confusing translations. (symbol: order_mismatch_objverb)
khd:
[SYNTAX]
- Reordering to VNeg targets; negation mis-placement causing misunderstanding of negation scope. (symbol: KHD NEG NEGV)
kls:
[SYNTAX]
- Direct translation produces incorrect verb placement. (symbol: order‑VO‑to‑OV)
kus:
[SYNTAX]
- Same as above – misplaced case markers. (symbol: postposition becomes preposition)
kwb:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Transliteration into languages that distinguish voiced/voiceless consonants collapses minimal pairs, causing homophony and possible meaning loss. (symbol: voicing:none‑loss)
lbc:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Implosive consonants may be replaced by the nearest stop, turning minimal‑pair words into homophones and breaking accurate translation. (symbol: phon:implosive‑loss)
lek:
[SYNTAX]
- Potential misreading of quantitative information (Same as Washo). (symbol: numer_pre_to_post) Exception: as above
lev:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Affixal targets may misplace plural marker or fail to capture the separateness of the plural word; plural-word may be omitted or mis-placed, yielding number-neutral translation or double marking. (symbol: LEV PLUR WORD)
lkr:
[SYNTAX]
- Highly specific constituent order; translating to SVO languages requires total restructuring of the clause to maintain the Oblique's relationship to the Verb. (symbol: lkr>>OVX-order)
mde:
[SYNTAX]
- Translation systems using the wrong type may place negation incorrectly relative to the verb, breaking polarity and clause structure. (symbol: [mde]>>sovneg:type-mismatch) Exception: WALS coding error noted
mle:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- When mapping to analytic languages, one of the two possessive markers may be omitted, causing ambiguity about the possessor’s grammatical role. (symbol: possessive prefix suffix)
mpy:
[OTHER]
- Loss of anatomical precision in target languages without the split. (symbol: hand arm distinction)
mqs:
[SYNTAX]
- Translating to postpositional languages requires reordering of adposition and noun phrase. (symbol: prep:vo:harmonic)
myj:
[SYNTAX]
- If the second negation marker is dropped, the sentence’s polarity flips or the intended emphatic negation is lost, producing incorrect or misleading translations. (symbol: double negation hazard)
[OTHER]
- Finger/hand/arm distinctions are lost or mis‑assigned if the Myene (mye) profile is loaded instead of Mayangna (myj). (symbol: myj>>iso-lexicon-mismatch)
nbd:
[SYNTAX]
- Misapplying identical predication patterns can collapse location meaning, causing ambiguous statements. (symbol: predicative distinct forms)
nbk:
[SYNTAX]
- Verb‑final language forbids clause‑final negation; negative morpheme must attach pre‑verbally within a complex verb template — translation from SOVNeg languages requires morphological intrusion into the verb complex. (symbol: neg:verb-template-intrude)
nmc:
[SYNTAX]
- Negative prefix appears between object and verb; translating from SNegVO or SVONeg languages requires reordering the negation morpheme across the object boundary while maintaining its prefixal attachment. (symbol: neg:prefix-sonv)
ntj:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translating into head‑marking languages leaves the possessed noun unmarked, breaking clarity of possession. (symbol: dep‑poss‑mark)
nyo:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translators may inadvertently add a second negative morpheme or shift the prefix, producing ungrammatical or meaning‑reversing sentences. (symbol: [nyo]>>neg:pref-noDou)
nyq:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Loss of the implosive feature may merge distinct phonemes, obscuring lexical distinctions in translation. (symbol: IMPLOSIVE ONLY HAZ)
oca:
[OTHER]
- Translating into languages with a single term may over‑generalize, losing the finer granularity of body‑part reference. (symbol: finger hand distinction)
ont:
[OTHER]
- When translating into languages that separate “hand” and “arm”, ambiguity may lead to semantic loss or mistranslation of medical/technical texts. (symbol: hand:arm:merge)
org:
[SYNTAX]
- Translators may omit overt case markers, leaving the negated argument unclear and potentially reversing meaning. (symbol: ARGMARK_SYM_NEG)
orx:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Case system identical to Evenki; lack of gender concord causes agreement failures when translating from gendered Altaic languages. (symbol: case:genderless-tungusic)
quy:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- When target script fails to copy reduplicated segment, lexical or grammatical nuance (e.g., intensification, plural) is omitted. (symbol: REDUP ORTHO LOSS)
rac:
[SYNTAX]
- Omitting the copula when translating into zero‑copula languages (e.g., Russian) yields incomplete clauses lacking a linking verb. (symbol: copula‑required)
rki:
[SYNTAX]
- Written translation loses interrogative force unless punctuation added; spoken translation to particle languages (Japanese ‑ka, Mandarin ma) requires morpheme insertion; intonation‑only strategy fails in low‑prosody channels (text, sign). (symbol: rki>>polq:inton-only)
sg:
[SYNTAX]
- Negation particle placed on wrong side of verb. (symbol: negation order conflict)
slk:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Etymological alignment and cognate detection must treat Slovak “čaj” as belonging to the *cha* borrowing zone. (symbol: slk>>lex:tea-cha-root)
srq:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translations from inflectional languages must map plural markers to a standalone lexical placeholder. Translating from a language that marks plurality with suffixes will omit the required plural lexical item in Siroi, causing loss of number marking. (symbol: [srq]>>plural:lexical-word)
tay:
[PHONOLOGY]
- Excluding uvular fricatives may erase phonemic contrasts needed for accurate modeling. (symbol: uvular:inventory:incomplete)
- Borrowed uvular fricatives must be mapped to stops or omitted, potentially altering lexical meaning. (symbol: tay>>uvu:stops-only)
tfr:
[SYNTAX]
- Incorrect polarity resolution yields affirmative meaning where negation intended, or vice versa. (symbol: [tfr]>>neg:pred+indef)
tio:
[SYNTAX]
- Single‑neg source becomes ungrammatical or flips meaning; an extra negative in the target language creates ungrammaticality or a polarity error. (symbol: NEGDOUBLEOB)
tkm:
[SYNTAX]
- Every prepositional phrase from a prepositional source language must be mirrored (NP → Postposition), disrupting constituency and potentially scope of modifiers. (symbol: [tkm]>>adp:post)
tlf:
[OTHER]
- Translation into languages with finger/hand identity will result in lexical collapse. (symbol: FINGERHAND_DISTINCT)
tmv:
[SYNTAX]
- Machine‑translation systems that assume prenominal order will misplace relative clauses, producing ungrammatical or garden‑path outputs. (symbol: [tmv]>>rel:postnominal-dom)
toz:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- The negative marker can be dropped or misplaced, leading to erroneous affirmative statements. (symbol: NEG PRE VERB)
tpm:
[SYNTAX]
- Automatic clause‑ordering may place subjects, objects, or verbs incorrectly, leading to ambiguity or grammatical errors in the target. (symbol: WORDORDERUNSTABLE)
vas:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Color adjective agreement must operate without gender classes; case endings alone mark syntactic relations on color‑modified nouns, reducing disambiguation cues in dense noun‑phrase structures. (symbol: color:case-only-agree)
wnk:
[SYNTAX]
- Potential misinterpretation of who does what to whom, resulting in semantic errors in translation. (symbol: freeOObl_ambig) Exception: languages employing overt case markers mitigate ambiguity
yid:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translators inserting a required noun (as in many target languages) create redundant or unnatural structures; omitting noun when target expects it leads to incomprehensible clauses. (symbol: adj‑standalone‑error)
ywr:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Same as above – improper negation placement. (symbol: prefix negation suffix)
zam:
[SYNTAX]
- Zero‑copula source sentences (Russian "Он врач", Arabic "هو طبيب") require copula insertion, increasing syllable count and altering information structure; omission yields ungrammatical output. (symbol: zam>>copula:oblig)
zku:
[OTHER]
- Translation may default to generic ‘hand’, losing proximal/distal contrast. (symbol: hand arm distinction)
zpm:
[MORPHOLOGY]
- Translating to symmetric negation languages (e.g., English) forces merger of non-realis and categorical negation, obscuring modality/evidentiality nuances. (symbol: neg:subtype-collapse)
zrs:
[SYNTAX]
- Translation to VO languages requires total reversal of core clause constituents. (symbol: zrs>>OV-order)
